title: SQL for Absolute Beginners
description: Normalized course plan generated from PDF curriculum input.

modules.title: Getting Started with SQL and Databases
modules.purpose: Build the learner's mental model before syntax: what SQL is, how databases organize information, and how a question becomes a query.
learningObjectives: Explain what SQL does in plain language.
Identify rows, columns, tables, and simple keys.
Describe how data questions map to database queries.
guidedExercises: Label the parts of a sample table: table name, columns, rows, and key candidate.
Translate three business questions into data questions.
Run a provided starter query inside a SQL editor and describe the result.
quizFocus: Define database, table, row, and column.
Explain why spreadsheets and relational tables are similar but not identical.
Identify when data should live in more than one table.
modules.moduleProject: Create a one-page data map for a tiny school or store database showing 3-4 tables and what each table stores.

modules.title: Your First Queries with SELECT
modules.purpose: Teach learners to retrieve data confidently with SELECT and FROM before filtering or joining tables.
learningObjectives: Write valid SELECT statements against a single table.
Choose between selecting all columns and selecting only needed columns.
Read result sets without confusion.
guidedExercises: Write four SELECT statements using one table and different column combinations.
Predict the output before running each query.
Correct five broken beginner queries.
quizFocus: What is the role of SELECT?
What is the role of FROM?
Why is SELECT * useful for exploration but risky in real applications?
modules.moduleProject: Build a one-page query sheet with ten starter SELECT queries against a student, product, or movie table.

modules.title: Filtering Data with WHERE
modules.purpose: Introduce decision-making in queries by teaching learners to keep only the rows they actually need.
learningObjectives: Use WHERE to narrow results correctly.
Compare text and numeric values using SQL operators.
Combine logical conditions without changing the intended meaning.
guidedExercises: Find all rows that match an exact value.
Filter a table using at least one range condition and one text condition.
Rewrite mixed AND/OR logic with parentheses to make it explicit.
quizFocus: What happens if a query has no WHERE clause?
When should you use AND versus OR?
Why do parentheses help when multiple conditions are involved?
modules.moduleProject: Create a 'data detective' worksheet with ten filtering prompts and answer them with SQL.

topics.title: Filter by more than one rule

modules.title: Sorting and Limiting Results
modules.purpose: Help beginners control output so answers are easier to read, rank, and compare.
learningObjectives: Sort result sets in meaningful ways.
Use LIMIT to keep only the most relevant rows.
Combine sorting and limiting to answer ranking questions.
guidedExercises: Show the top 5 most expensive products.
Show the 10 most recent rows in a table with dates.
Sort by two columns and explain how ties are handled.
quizFocus: What is the default sort direction in most SQL engines?
Why is LIMIT often paired with ORDER BY?
What does a secondary sort do?
modules.moduleProject: Build a 'top performers' report for a sample sales or grades dataset.

modules.title: Working with Text, Lists, Ranges, and NULL
modules.purpose: Expand filtering to real-world cases like partial text, multiple choices, ranges, and missing values.
guidedExercises: Find names that start with a given letter.
Find rows whose category is in a list of allowed values.
Identify rows with missing contact information.
quizFocus: Why does = NULL not work the way beginners expect?
What is the difference between % and _ in LIKE patterns?
When is IN cleaner than multiple OR conditions?
modules.moduleProject: Create a search-and-cleanup worksheet for a customer table with missing or inconsistent information.

modules.title: Calculated Columns and SQL Expressions
modules.purpose: Teach learners to create new values from existing data so raw tables become useful reports.
learningObjectives: Use arithmetic and expressions inside SELECT.
Rename outputs with aliases for readability.
Apply simple built-in functions safely.
guidedExercises: Calculate line totals from price and quantity.
Create a discounted price column.
Use aliases to make a result set presentation-ready.
quizFocus: What is an expression in SQL?
Why are aliases useful in reports?
What happens when an expression uses NULL values?
modules.moduleProject: Build a simple invoice or payroll report with calculated columns and clear output names.

modules.title: Aggregate Functions
modules.purpose: Move from row-by-row reading to summarizing many rows into a single business answer.
learningObjectives: Use core aggregate functions correctly.
Summarize filtered subsets of data.
Interpret aggregate output in business terms.
guidedExercises: Count the number of customers in a table.
Find total revenue for a filtered set of orders.
Calculate average score or salary for a chosen group of rows.
quizFocus: What is the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column)?
Why are aggregates useful for dashboards and reporting?
How does WHERE interact with an aggregate query?
modules.moduleProject: Create a one-page KPI report for a tiny business dataset using only aggregate queries.

modules.title: Grouping Data with GROUP BY and HAVING
modules.purpose: Teach learners to summarize by category, person, team, or product instead of only summarizing the whole table.
learningObjectives: Write grouped summaries with GROUP BY.
Choose between WHERE and HAVING based on the stage of filtering.
Explain grouped result sets clearly.
guidedExercises: Count orders by customer.
Find average salary by department.
Show only groups that exceed a chosen threshold using HAVING.
quizFocus: Why can't HAVING fully replace WHERE?
What happens when a selected column is neither grouped nor aggregated?
Why does GROUP BY make result sets shorter than the original table?
modules.moduleProject: Create a management summary report with grouped counts, sums, and averages.

modules.title: Introduction to Joins
modules.purpose: Show how relational databases connect information stored in different tables.
learningObjectives: Explain why related data is split across tables.
Write INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN queries.
Identify missing matches and interpret NULLs after joins.
guidedExercises: Join customers to orders and show a combined report.
Use LEFT JOIN to find rows that do not have a match.
Sketch the relationship before writing the query.
quizFocus: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?
Why are keys important in join logic?
Why do NULL values appear in some left-joined results?
modules.moduleProject: Build a multi-table report that combines at least three tables from a school, store, or HR dataset.

modules.title: Writing Cleaner Queries
modules.purpose: Improve readability so beginners do not just write working SQL, but SQL they can revisit, debug, and teach from.
learningObjectives: Use aliases and formatting to improve clarity.
Write SQL that is easy to debug and review.
Add comments only when they genuinely help understanding.
guidedExercises: Reformat three one-line queries into readable SQL.
Rename poor aliases to clearer ones.
Add concise comments to a query with non-obvious logic.
quizFocus: When are table aliases helpful versus unnecessary?
Why should SQL be broken across lines?
What makes a query easy for another person to maintain?
modules.moduleProject: Take a messy starter script and refactor it into a clean, readable reporting query pack.

modules.title: Subqueries for Beginners
modules.purpose: Introduce query-inside-query thinking without overwhelming learners with advanced database theory.
learningObjectives: Read subqueries from the inside out.
Use simple subqueries in WHERE and FROM.
Recognize when a join might be clearer than a subquery.
guidedExercises: Find products priced above the overall average.
Use IN with a subquery to find customers who placed orders.
Wrap a grouped query and filter it in an outer query.
quizFocus: Why are aliases important for subqueries in FROM?
What does it mean to read a subquery inside out?
When might a join be easier to understand?
modules.moduleProject: Build a comparison worksheet showing two solutions to the same problem: one with a join and one with a subquery.

topics.title: Build on top of another query

modules.title: Inserting Data
modules.purpose: Shift from reading data to adding new rows carefully and correctly.
guidedExercises: Insert one valid row into a table.
Insert multiple rows in a single statement.
Check the table afterward to confirm what changed.
quizFocus: Why is it safer to name columns in INSERT statements?
What happens if a required column is missing?
When can NULL be inserted intentionally?
modules.moduleProject: Create a seed-data script that loads starter data for a simple student, product, or book database.

modules.title: Updating and Deleting Data
modules.purpose: Teach safe data changes and the habit of previewing before modifying records.
learningObjectives: Update and delete rows intentionally.
Preview affected rows before running changes.
Adopt safe habits that prevent accidental data loss.
guidedExercises: Run a SELECT preview for rows you plan to update.
Update a filtered subset of rows.
Delete only temporary or test rows after confirming the target set.
quizFocus: Why should UPDATE and DELETE almost always be paired with WHERE?
What is a safe preview workflow before data changes?
Why is accidental deletion more dangerous than a bad SELECT query?
modules.moduleProject: Write a maintenance script that updates statuses, fixes one field, and removes obsolete test data using safe preview steps.

modules.title: Creating Tables and Database Structure
modules.purpose: Introduce schema design so learners can build small databases instead of only querying prebuilt ones.
learningObjectives: Create basic relational tables with sensible data types.
Use core constraints to protect data quality.
Explain why schema choices affect future queries.
guidedExercises: Design a students table and justify each column.
Create a products table with a primary key and price column.
Link two tables with a foreign key.
quizFocus: What does a primary key guarantee?
Why is NOT NULL useful?
How does a foreign key support relational design?
modules.moduleProject: Design and create the full schema for a tiny business or school database with at least three related tables.

modules.title: Relational Database Design for Beginners
modules.purpose: Strengthen the modeling side of SQL so learners understand why tables are shaped the way they are.
learningObjectives: Recognize common relationship patterns.
Reduce duplication by separating repeated information.
Turn a real-world scenario into related tables.
guidedExercises: Identify relationship types in three short scenarios.
Redesign a denormalized table into multiple related tables.
Draw a basic entity-relationship sketch before writing SQL.
quizFocus: What is the difference between one-to-many and many-to-many?
Why is duplicate data dangerous?
Why should tables stay focused on one main subject?
modules.moduleProject: Create a beginner ERD and matching CREATE TABLE script for a chosen domain such as school, bookstore, clinic, or art store.

modules.title: Real Beginner SQL Projects and Course Capstone
modules.purpose: Consolidate everything into realistic beginner projects that require reading, filtering, grouping, joining, and basic data design.
learningObjectives: Combine multiple beginner SQL skills in a single workflow.
Plan, build, query, and explain a small relational database.
Finish the course with portfolio-ready beginner projects.
guidedExercises: Choose one domain and create starter tables plus sample data.
Answer at least ten business questions with SQL.
Present the final project as a short written report with queries and explanations.
quizFocus: Which SQL skills appear most often in real reports?
How do you decide what tables and columns a project needs?
Why is being able to explain a query just as important as writing it?
modules.moduleProject: Complete a capstone database project with schema, seed data, 10-15 queries, one grouped report, one multi-table join report, and a brief written explanation.
